payload.summary: Both amendments identify genuine gaps. Amendment fr-5-f1c7b7b6 correctly identifies that legacy rows with plan_hash=None are never repaired even when the snapshot hash hasn't changed since the row was created — the blanket prohibition conflates two distinct scenarios. Amendment fr-5-39bc594e correctly identifies that the round-trip tests are example-based and cannot detect new optional fields added with skip_serializing_if, plus the duplicated schema remains a maintenance hazard.

votes.rationale: The code at lines 2927-2931 and 3257-3261 applies a blanket prohibition against snapshot-based plan_hash backfill for all existing rows, but the stated rationale (avoid mislabeling a v1 attempt as v2 when persist_plan() advances the snapshot hash) only applies to one of two distinct cases. Case 1: the snapshot hash has advanced past the attempt's original plan version — backfilling would mislabel, so skipping is correct. Case 2: a legacy row was created before auto-population existed (plan_hash=None) and the snapshot hash has NOT changed since — this is a safe repair opportunity that is currently missed. The new auto-population at line 2958-2960 only runs for brand-new inserts, and backfill_running_entry at line 2733-2748 only fills plan_hash when the caller explicitly provides it. If callers keep omitting plan_hash on retry/completion calls, legacy rows complete with plan_hash=None permanently. The fix is straightforward: when an existing row has plan_hash=None, compare the snapshot hash against the row's started_at or other provenance to determine if backfill is safe, rather than blanket-skipping. This is a real defect in the repair/migration path for Issue 3.

The round-trip tests at lines 3768-3812 populate all fields with Some values and verify the round-trip succeeds, but they cannot structurally detect drift. The critical failure mode: if a new field is added to StartJournalDetailsPayload or CompletionJournalDetailsPayload in model.rs with `skip_serializing_if = Option::is_none` and defaults to None, the render function will never emit it when it's None, so the fs adapter's deny_unknown_fields won't trigger and the round-trip test passes perfectly — despite the fs struct lacking the field entirely. The test only catches drift when a new REQUIRED field is added or when a test case happens to set the new optional field to Some. Meanwhile the duplicated structs (fs.rs lines 2279-2339 vs model.rs lines 353-386) remain a concrete maintenance hazard. A structural guard — such as a compile-time assertion that both types implement the same trait, or a test that serializes a payload with ALL optional fields set to Some — would close this gap. The current example-based approach gives a false sense of safety.
